data.title: Tariff Decision Day?
data.description:
The US dollar (DXY) is trading mostly firmer but quietly in what could be a volatile North American session. It is not just about the US data, of which there is plenty - including the PCE deflator and the first look at Q4 GDP, the preliminary February PMI and the University of Michigan’s final February reading on consumer confidence. The Supreme Court will hand down some decisions today, and it's possible that it rules on the president’s use of emergency powers to impose widespread tariffs. The event markets suggest a strong majority expect the high court to rule against most all of the tariffs that have been justified by the International Economic Emergency Powers Act (IEEPA). Tensions between the US and Iran remain elevated, but the threat of an imminent strike has relaxed a little and oil prices are a little softer ahead of the weekend.

The dollar has risen against the G10 currencies this week. The yen has been the weakest, and it is off almost 1.65%. The Australian dollar has been the best performer, and it is off about 0.3% this week. Most emerging market currencies also declined this week. The Argentine peso (~0.6%), Russian ruble (~0.2%), and Brazilian real (0.15%) have been the best performers.

Prices

G10

• The euro was sold to almost $1.1740 in North America yesterday, a four-week low. It has held barely above it today. After the low was recorded, the euro was unable to resurface above $1.1780, around Wednesday’s low, and this is also holding. Options for one billion euros at $1.1775 expire today. The next area of chart support is in the $1.1670-1.1700 area. The euro settled slightly below $1.1870 at the end of last week.

• The dollar reached a seven-session high against the Japanese yen today near JPY155.65 in late Asia/early European turnover. It has come off to around JPY155.15 in the European morning. The session low was near JPY154.90. Still, the dollar’s technical tone is constructive, and overcoming the JPY155.65-80 area could target the JPY156.50-65 area next.

• Sterling was sold to a four-week low yesterday near $1.3435. While fraying the 200-day moving average (~$1.3445), sterling met the (50%) retracement of its 8.5-cent rally from early last November’s low (~$1.3010) to the late-January high (~$1.3865). Despite the strong retail sales and firm PMI, it has barely benefited. Cable has held below $1.3485. It must resurface above $1.3510 to improve the technical tone. The next area of support is seen around $1.3400 and then $1.3340-50. Sterling’s roughly 1.3% loss this week, if sustained today, would be the largest since January 2025.

• The US dollar traded at its best level against the Canadian dollar in nearly two weeks shortly after Canada reported a smaller-than-expected December merchandise trade deficit and the US reported a somewhat larger deficit. Still, excluding the pandemic distortion, Canada’s trade shortfall (~C$313 bln) was a record. Overall exports fell by 0.2%, led by a 5.8% drop in exports to the US. The greenback reached CAD1.3715 and remained above about CAD1.3680 for the remainder of the session. That range is largely holding today, but the Canadian dollar still looks vulnerable.

• A hawkish-leaning central bank and labor market data helped the Australian dollar minimize its losses against the firmer US dollar. With about a 0.35% decline this week, it is the best-performing G10 currency against the dollar. It kissed the 20-day moving average ($0.7025) yesterday for the first time in a month. It extended the losses to $0.7015 today before it recovered to a little above $0.7065. The momentum indicators are turning lower, warning of continued near-term consolidation. A break of the $0.6990-0.7000 level would suggest an outright correction is at hand.

EM

• After setting a new low against the Mexican peso since mid-2024 on Wednesday near MXN17.0865, the greenback has recovered. It poked above MXN17.30 yesterday for the first time in almost two weeks and settled above the 20-day moving average (~MXN17.26). It is consolidating quietly ahead of the North American session. Most of the Latam currencies fell this week, with the Argentine peso a notable exception. The month’s high was recorded on February 2 (~MXN17.5725).

• The dollar forged a base against the offshore yuan ahead of CNH6.88 in the first few days of the week. It approached Monday’s high yesterday as it traded near CNH6.9055. The greenback is in an exceptionally narrow range today, ~CNH6.8975-6.9045. It was trading around CNH6.90 when the mainland markets shut for the extended holiday.

• After yesterday’s holiday, the Indian rupee was sold to a new low for the month, and the central bank reportedly intervened in both the onshore and offshore markets. The dollar opened well above the recent highs and rose to INR90.9925, its best level since February 2. The rise in oil prices weighed on the local bond market as well as the currency.

Other Markets

• Equities are mixed today. In the Asia-Pacific region, while Tokyo (NKY:IND) lost over 1%, the surge in South Korea’s Kospi (KOSPI) continued. It rose 2.3% today to bring this week’s advance to 9.5%. Hong Kong (HSI) and mainland shares that trade there fell by 1.10-1.20%. Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) is firm, recovering about half of yesterday’s 0.55% loss. US index futures (SPX) are narrowly mixed.

• Benchmark 10-year yields are mostly lower. The soft national CPI saw JGBs extend their rally. The yield has fallen for four consecutive weeks. It is off 10 bp this week, the most since April 2025. European yields are mostly lower, led by the two-basis point decline in UK Gilts. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is flat, near 4.07%. It is up about two basis points ahead of US data.

• Gold rose to a new high for the week near $5042.50, which is slightly above last week’s settlement. It is near $5027 in late European morning turnover. The same is true of silver. It reached a new high for the week, near $81.20, and is around $80.50.

• April WTI edged higher initially, extending yesterday’s gain, and poked briefly above $67, where sellers emerged and pushed it back to around $66. It settled at $62.75 last week. Many are trying to game out the timing of attack scenarios, trying to take into account the lunar phase for ambient light, the Ramadan holiday (extends to March 19/20), and the like.

Data

• There are four highlights for the US today. The first estimate for Q4 GDP is due, and the median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey has crept up to 3% at an annualized rate. The price deflators are expected to have softened. The December personal income and consumption data are due with the deflators, which are expected to have held steady or ticked up slightly. The preliminary February PMI is due, and the composite looks little changed around 53.0. The last highlight, but it could be the most market-impactful, is the Supreme Court’s decision day. It does not pre-announce which cases it will rule on, but there is a chance that the verdict about the president’s use of emergency powers to levy widespread tariffs will be handed down.

• Canada reports December retail sales. The headline was likely dragged down by weaker vehicle sales.

• Mexico also reports December retail sales. After a 1.0% jump in November, sales likely slipped a little at the end of the year.

• The preliminary February eurozone PMI improved. The manufacturing PMI rose from 49.5 to 50.8, its highest reading since June 2022. New orders rose to 50.9 from 49.2, which is the highest since April 2022. However, new export orders remained below the 50 boom/bust level for the eighth consecutive month. Germany’s manufacturing PMI rose above 50 for the first time in three and a half years, while the French manufacturing PMI slipped back below 50 (49.9) after holding above 50 for the previous two months. The aggregate service PMI edged up for the first time in three months (51.9 from 51.6). Last year’s peak was in November at 53.6. The composite PMI rose to 51.9 from 51.3 in January.

• The busy week for UK data ended today with an unexpected jump in retail sales and mixed preliminary February PMI. January retail sales jumped 1.8% (median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey was for a 0.2% increase) after December’s 0.4% increase. Excluding gasoline, retail sales surged 2.0% following December’s 0.3% rise. The flash composite PMI ticked up 53.9 from 53.7 in January, which was the strongest since April 2024. The manufacturing PMI firmed to 52.0 from 51.8, while services growth eased slightly (53.9 vs. 54.0). When everything has been said and done, the swaps market anticipates a year-end rate of about 3.23%, which implies two cuts this year.

• Australia’s preliminary February PMI softened. The composite eased to 52.0 from 55.7 in January. It was 51.0 at the end of last year and 50.6 in February 2025. The manufacturing PMI stands at 51.5 (52.3 in January), while the service PMI is at 52.2 (56.3 in January).

• The Tokyo CPI that was reported a few weeks ago signaled the decline in Japan’s January CPI. The headline rate fell to 1.5% from 2.1%, and the core rate, which excludes fresh food, fell to the 2.0% target from 2.4% in December. Excluding fresh food and energy, consumer inflation remained sticky at 2.6% (2.9% in December). Separately, the preliminary February composite PMI rose to 53.8 from 53.1 in January. The manufacturing PMI is at 52.8 (51.5 in January), and the services PMI stands at 53.8 (53.7 in January).

• India’s February PMIs continued to show a strong start to the year. The composite PMI is 59.3 (58.4 in January), which is higher than the manufacturing PMI (57.5) and the services PMI (58.4). The Reserve Bank of India projects the economy will expand by 7.3% in the current fiscal year, up from 6.5% in FY25.

data.title: John Hancock 2055 Lifetime Blend Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year-end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The fund's overweight in equities versus bonds contributed to relative performance. An overweight in emerging-market stocks, the best-performing segment of the global equity markets in the quarter, was a further plus. On the other hand, the allocation to defensive equities was a modest detractor at a time in which more speculative stocks were in favor. An overweight in U.S. mid caps also cost the fund some relative performance. Positioning in fixed income had a neutral effect on results. An allocation to emerging-market bonds performed well, as the asset class strongly outperformed its developed-market peers in an environment of falling interest rates and rising commodity prices. Conversely, a position in U.S. Treasury STRIPS detracted amid a broader increase in long-term bond yields.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund's active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets, while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund's positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it's impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.

data.title: Star Holdings: A Liquidating Security Approaching Capital Returns Phase
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the McIntyre Partnerships Q4 2025 Partners Letter.

Star Holdings (STHO) is a liquidating security in which the fund has been invested since 2023. Its primary assets are 13.3MM SAFE shares and liquidating real estate in Asbury Park and Richmond. It is a very low-liquidity security, where the stock's price and fundamental value can move in opposite directions purely due to liquidity. After several years with minimal updates from the company, I believe that has happened here. However, STHO has made significant progress toward liquidation over the past three years, and I believe the company will announce significant capital returns in 2026.

When it was spun off from its former parent, iStar, now called Safehold (SAFE), STHO was envisioned as a three- to five-year liquidation, with certain hard-to-sell assets placed in the spinco to simplify the larger company's investment story. We are now entering year four of this plan. These assets were not spun off because they were troubled, as is typical in "bad bank" spins, but rather because they were real estate developments that do not match SAFE's mandate to invest in ground leases. Since the spin, STHO has made significant progress, realizing sales of ~$170MM of real estate versus their book value of ~$120MM. STHO now holds only $113MM of land for development, down from $224MM at the time of spin. Further, public filings in Asbury Park indicate that several land parcels have been optioned by large builders such as Hovnanian and Toll Brothers, yet they remain on STHO's balance sheet. I anticipate most of these lots will move towards construction in 2026. STHO has also substantially reduced the management fee owed to SAFE, from ~$55MM at the time of spin to ~$10MM today. To facilitate these asset sales, STHO engaged in numerous seller financings. While initially a drain on cash, STHO has successfully recuperated all of these when due, and I anticipate several key repayments in 2026, including the elimination of the Surfhouse JV, which has made STHO's financials difficult to understand. These actions have substantially strengthened STHO's balance sheet. At year-end 2025, I calculate STHO's cash and readily marketable bond investments at ~$94MM versus their credit facility at $115MM.

Which brings us to STHO's largest and most important asset – its 13.3MM SAFE shares. At the time of the spin, STHO had significant asset value but limited cash. To safely capitalize the spinco, management spun it off with shares of the parent company, SAFE, which could be sold if needed. As I calculate STHO at ~$21MM of net debt, versus my expectations for >$50MM of cash flow from loan repayments and asset sales in 2026, the logic for maintaining their significant SAFE stake is weakening. I anticipate STHO announcing either a spin-off or sale of their SAFE shares in 2026, along with a repayment or restructuring of their existing credit facility. As STHO's SAFE shares have a current value of ~$120MM, net of a $91MM margin loan, a distribution to shareholders would be ~$10 per share, versus STHO's current $8 price.

While some investors may question whether management is committed to returning capital, hence the discount in STHO's shares, I have had positive discussions with management. They are also significant shareholders. CEO Jay Sugarman owns ~4% of shares outstanding. More importantly, STHO is already returning capital to shareholders via share buybacks. In 2025, STHO repurchased ~9% of shares outstanding.

If STHO continues to make progress and returns capital to shareholders, I believe our ultimate realization for STHO should be $20-$30/sh. versus a current price of $8.

data.title: QQQM: Capturing AI Growth Doesn't Have To Be Complicated
data.description:
Overview

If you're a follower of mine, you'll know that I almost exclusively publish analyses related to dividend stocks and high-yield funds. I previously published an article titled 'A Warning To Young Dividend Investors', which highlighted some of the mistakes I made along my investing journey from a decade ago. As a dividend investor, you may dismiss many high quality companies that don't offer the best dividend value. However, I believe that ignoring the potential growth of the technology sector in this era of AI would be a mistake. Therefore, I wanted to cover the Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF (QQQM), which I believe to be one of the most straightforward ways to participate in the expansion of AI.

Looking at the performance of the ETF, we can see that QQQM has delivered a total return of more than 12.3% over the last twelve months. The fund offers a small dividend yield of 0.5% while providing exposure to many of the highest quality companies in the world. However, QQQM is the younger sibling to the more popular Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). Despite this, I believe that QQQM is the better holding for long-term investors that want to implement a buy-and-hold strategy.

Sentiment in the technology sector can shift quickly so my approach has always been to exclusively remain exposed to the large-cap players in the space. These tend to be businesses with diversified revenue streams that can produce consistent cash flows to help navigate any headwinds that AI faces. This reduces the speculative nature of the sector and makes it a lot less stressful for investors. Although I am bullish on technology, I believe the path forward will be filled with uncertainty and periods of elevated volatility, like we are currently experiencing. However, QQQM is a great way to navigate potential risks since it provides a healthy level of diversity across the Nasdaq-100 index.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, QQQM has total assets under management of $71.1B that are spread across the highest quality businesses in the index. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 0.15%, which makes it slightly more cost-efficient than QQQ's expense ratio of 0.20%. There isn't much of a difference between QQQM and QQQ, except for the size of the funds and their expense ratio. QQQ may present better liquidity and the ability to trade options against it, but in terms of strategy and holdings the funds look quite similar.

Under normal market conditions, the fund will invest at least 90% of its assets into stocks within the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX). QQQM rebalances its holdings quarterly, while initiating reconstitution of its holdings annually. Since the fund essentially mirrors the index, the allocation by sector looks similar. For instance, QQQM is most heavily allocated to the technology sector at 51.62% of assets. I actually like the fact that QQQM isn't entirely allocated to technology stocks because this helps the fund participate in the sector's growth but with the potential for less volatility. This is followed by an allocation to communication and consumer cyclical companies, which accounts for 15.89% and 12.82% of assets respectively.

When looking at the top holdings of QQQM, we can see that the ETF is a bit top heavy. Although QQQM has exposure to 102 different positions, the top ten holdings make up ~49.5% of total assets. For instance, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest holding with a weight of 9.04%. This is followed by exposure to many other high quality companies, including Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Tesla (TSLA), and Meta Platforms (META), just to name a few. As we can see, QQQM essentially provides exposure to the Mag 7, which means that investors can directly participate in the growth of these companies.

An ETF like QQQM is always an efficient long-term holding because an investor can instantly get diversity across the strongest businesses in the world. By investing in a basket of these companies, we can eliminate some of the speculation, risks, and guess work involved with selecting individual positions. This diversity also means that we get exposure to some of the strong momentum companies, such as Palantir (PLTR), which trade at massive premium valuations, but continue to rise over time due to their positive market sentiment.

Outlook

It's hard to talk about the outlook of technology and AI without coming across as hyperbolic. However, I truly believe that this is one of the most interesting opportunities that the market has provided in the last decade. From a consumer's perspective, I already enjoy the many forms of advances that were influenced by AI within my entertainment avenues like video games or movies, while also seeing how LLMs (Large Language Models) like Gemini or ChatGPT can be used to enhance productivity and creativity within various forms of media.

From an investor's perspective, I believe that the growth of companies may not necessarily come from the products or services that are the result of AI. While this already does exist, such as Alphabet's (GOOG) Gemini subscription revenue, I believe that the real potential is how AI will improve operating efficiency for companies. I believe that we will see companies efficiently decouple their headcount from the revenue growth and costs will be cut to levels that were once unimaginable. While this may be some years away, the idea isn't too far-fetched as we already see plenty of examples of this.

I personally believe that Amazon will be one of the clearest examples of this over time. The business already has great momentum with the 24% growth of its AWS segment, but I think their recent restructuring will serve as the blueprint that many other companies will follow. For instance, Amazon has initiated several rounds of layoffs to trim capital as their capex spending increases. The business may be shifting to focus on capital efficiency, which has the potential to massively improve their operating margins. CEO, Andy Jassy, has already stated that generative AI and agents will replace the workforce. If Amazon is successful in their execution, we may see other large caps follow their lead.

We will need fewer people doing some of the jobs that are being done today, and more people doing other types of jobs. -- It’s hard to know exactly where this nets out over time, but in the next few years, we expect that this will reduce our total corporate workforce as we get efficiency gains from using AI extensively across the company.

Furthermore, the projected growth of the size of AI sector looks attractive. At the close of 2025, the size of the AI market was valued around $391B. Grand View Research currently estimates that the size of the AI market can increase as high as ~$3.5T by 2033. This represents a strong CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% through 2033. Even if this estimate is half-wrong, this would still represent a massive double-digit growth potential over the next several years. Since QQQM has direct exposure to the businesses leading the AI charge forward, this is one of the clearest ways for investors to participate.

Risks

As we've seen over the last few weeks, the market isn't a big fan of the massive capex spending levels that businesses are initiating. In order to fulfill the growing demand, many of the large-cap leaders are allocating record-breaking levels of cash towards the build out of their AI infrastructure. Just to help visualize how large spending levels have become, here are some estimated capex spending amounts from this most recent earnings season:

U.S. Hyperscale Capex Estimates (2026 Fiscal Guidance)

  • Amazon: ~$200B
  • Alphabet: ~$175B - ~$185B
  • Microsoft ~$140B
  • Meta Platforms: ~$115B - ~$135B

These massive capex spending levels have investors concerned on whether or not this will actually lead to higher earnings growth in the near-term. As a result, there has been a surge in short bets within the market. There's always the risk that these different AI initiatives take longer than anticipated or cost more than originally forecast.

Furthermore, data compiled by Evercore ISI Research indicates the plummeting free cash flow levels this capex spending has caused. This data says that the free cash flow levels have fallen to the 'yellow flag' areas that were present during the lows of the 2022 cycle. These analysts are essentially saying that they believe businesses need to substantially improve cash on their balance sheets, rather than committing more capital towards capex spending.

However, I believe these risks are a bit overstated since the Mag 7 companies that I just listed are some of the most profitable businesses in the world. I actually think that the front-loading of capital to build out their infrastructure can be a benefit. Companies tend not to aggressively increase capex unless they see a clear rise in demand or an end-goal. Additionally, many of these same businesses just reported healthy earnings, which I believe should offset concerns. For instance, META reported that its revenue increased 24%, beating expectations.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that QQQM remains a very straightforward way for investors to obtain exposure to some of the leaders within the technology sector. QQQM is cost-efficient with a low expense ratio and the diversity across businesses means that investors are able to participate in the expansion of the market with less risk than holding individual positions. The market seems to be worried about the large capex spending levels but I believe these risks are overdone. Many of the large-cap leaders in the sector continue to report strong earnings growth and the build out of AI infrastructure will better align these businesses to capture the rising demand of the sector.

data.title: Sotera Health: A Predictable And Growing Business
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the McIntyre Partnerships Q4 2025 Partners Letter.

Sotera Health's (SHC) stock was volatile in 2025 but ultimately finished strong. Despite the share price volatility, SHC remains a predictable, growing, and recession-proof business. These are exactly the attributes we look for in a core holding. Here are the company's financials over the last few years:

Despite the noise, the core business remains a stable franchise that has consistently grown sales and operating profits with zero down years in the last 20 years.

Further, 2025 marked an inflection year, with the core Sterigenics segment posting 10% revenue growth, up from ~4-7% in the previous two years. This inflection was driven by Sterigenics finally lapping the inventory destocking that hospitals and distributors saw following COVID-19 hoarding, which lifted volumes from flat to ~5% growth. With destocking in the rearview mirror, SHC is well positioned to return to its historical 10%+ EBITDA growth rate, which is above Street estimates and, I believe, represents a significant catalyst.

In addition, SHC should benefit from the tax law changes in the OBIB Act. SHC has been paying a ~34% tax rate because the 2017 tax law lowered the interest deductibility cap from 30% of EBITDA to 30% of EBIT in 2022. The new tax law restores deductibility to EBITDA, which, combined with SHC's growth and debt paydown, will reduce SHC's tax rate to the mid-twenties. However, despite the change in the law, the Street still models SHC's tax rate around 32%. When SHC reports, I anticipate guidance confirming the lowered tax rate, which should boost EPS estimates by ~10%.

SHC also saw solid developments on the legal front. They continued to make progress and settled numerous cases in line with previous settlements. Further, SHC saw a significant number of cases dismissed in Georgia for failure to prove causation, which should reduce the future value of settlements in the state. While I anticipate SHC will continue to face litigation and pay settlements, I believe the largest settlements are behind them, and further settlements, like the $31MM in April 2025, are minor bumps rather than material headwinds.

Despite improved volumes, changes in tax law, and positive legal developments, SHC remains at a material discount to STE, the other side of their sterilization duopoly. For comparison, STE trades at ~25x 2026 EPS and 21x 2027 EPS, versus SHC at 17x and 14x my estimates, respectively. Given the inflecting growth and discount to peers, we retain a large position. I believe our investment is positioned to benefit from SHC's ~10% EBITDA growth per year, continuing capital returns, and potential multiple expansion. I value SHC at 25x my 2027 EPS estimate of $1.20, implying a $30 valuation versus its current $17 price.

data.title: Fastly: Improved Demand, But The Company Remains Flawed
data.description:
Fastly's (FSLY) share price surged after the company reported Q4 2025 results that showed a surprisingly large growth acceleration. While Network Services revenue growth was solid, supported by emerging AI tailwinds, Security and edge compute are becoming increasingly important contributors.

I previously suggested that there were positive shifts occurring within Fastly's business. Despite a low valuation and potential near-term tailwinds, I was cautious about the stock, though, as Fastly is a flawed company due to its narrow platform vision and focus on relatively commoditized markets.

While pricing pressure has temporarily eased, the CDN market structure remains unchanged. In addition, Fastly continues to face customer concentration risk and a lack of customer growth. There is also a risk that Security growth will moderate as the boost provided by the adoption of newer products fades. As a result, I am fairly neutral on Fastly's prospects, despite the likelihood of strong growth and improving margins over the next 12 months.

Market Conditions

Internet traffic increased by an estimated 16% YoY in 2025, with growth supported by AI and agentic traffic. In addition, the shift of sports and other live events to streaming is a tailwind as it contributes to traffic spikes.

While CDN prices have historically declined 5% annually on average, Fastly has suggested that in recent years prices have declined by a double-digit percentage annually. A stronger demand environment and market consolidation have now created more supportive pricing conditions, though. This includes Edgio (formerly Limelight) filing for bankruptcy, Akamai (AKAM) acquiring Linode and Lumen, and StackPath selling their CDN assets. Price erosion in the fourth quarter was reportedly in the mid-single digits.

Investor focus is likely to be more on Fastly's security and edge compute businesses going forward, though, because of the opportunities that AI is creating (bot management, API security, edge inferencing). Fastly isn't as well positioned to benefit from AI and agent traffic as Cloudflare (NET), though, due to the company's fairly narrow platform vision. I believe that this is critical because of the margin benefits that diverse traffic provides.

Fastly Business Updates

Fastly introduced a bot mitigation solution and made its adaptive DDoS protection solution generally available in 2024, giving the company a complete security portfolio (bot mitigation, WAF, DDoS prevention). This now appears to be paying off, as the company's Security revenue growth has accelerated significantly over the past 12 months. Broader platform adoption should also help to strengthen the company's competitive positioning and reduce Network Services pricing pressure. There is a risk that this is just a one-off growth boost as adoption rises among existing customers, though. Even if this does prove to be the case, there should still be a considerable growth runway, as only a single-digit percentage of Fastly's customers had adopted its DDoS or bot mitigation solutions in early 2025.

Fastly's Other segment is also a growth driver at the moment, which largely relates to its edge compute solution. Compute enables Fastly to provide dynamic real-time experiences, particularly now that the company offers storage. Fastly believes that edge compute will support the expansion of its business in strategic verticals, like travel and leisure, technology, financial services, and retail.

Much of Fastly's recent focus has also been on a go-to-market transformation, designed to accelerate the acquisition of new logos. For example, Fastly introduced a self-service solution and is using mix-and-match packages with simplified pricing to drive adoption. Weak growth of the customer base should be viewed as particularly disappointing in light of this. The company also has a high-touch customer success motion for large media accounts, which is designed to drive traffic share growth among its largest customers. Strong Network Services and RPO growth indicate that Fastly is having more success with this initiative.

Financial Analysis

Fastly generated $172.6 million in revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of 23% YoY. Network Services growth is reportedly being driven by increased traffic and share gains within customers, as indicated by Fastly's strength relative to Akamai.

While growth was robust across Fastly's segments, Security is a growth driver at the moment, supported by customers adopting newer products. Edge compute growth also remains strong, although this is still a fairly small part of Fastly's business.

It is worth noting that Fastly lapped the loss of a large media company in the second quarter, meaning easier comparables have contributed to the improvement in growth.

While churn remains very low, Fastly still hasn't been able to meaningfully expand its customer base. The company had 3,092 total customers and 628 enterprise customers in the fourth quarter. This means that growth is coming from expansion within existing customers, as demonstrated by the company's 110% LTM NRR. On a positive note, Fastly has realized very large RPO increases in recent quarters, indicating that larger customers are committing to the platform.

Fastly is guiding to $168-174 million in revenue in the first quarter, representing an 18% YoY increase at the midpoint. For the full year, the company expects $700-720 million in revenue, representing 14% growth at the midpoint. Guidance is fairly soft, but Fastly isn't extrapolating recent trends due to macro uncertainty. This likely means that growth will handily beat expectations in the near term.

Infrastructure CapEx will rise to 10–12% of revenue in 2026, which supports the notion of strong growth in 2026. Fastly's hiring remains depressed, though, indicating that the company is still cautious about investing ahead of expected growth.

In addition to stronger growth, Fastly has been able to achieve impressive margin gains over the past few years. This is supported by Security and edge compute growth, both of which carry higher gross margins. Gross margin improvements have also been supported by a more favorable pricing environment for Network Services. While the CDN market has consolidated, a less robust demand environment could see pricing pressure return.

Fastly is targeting 75-80% of incremental revenue falling through to gross profit and 35-40% falling through to operating profit going forward, which should see the company transition to profitability over the next few years.

Conclusion

Fastly's share price has shot higher in recent weeks, supported by improving margins and a significant growth reacceleration. Growth is being driven by an improved demand environment and success in security products (WAF, Bot Management, etc.). Edge compute growth also remains robust, and this part of the business should become more material over the next few years.

While this may seem like a positive setup, Fastly continues to have a concentrated customer base, which it is struggling to expand. The company is also still heavily dependent on relatively commoditized Network Services. In addition, there is a risk that Security growth proves temporary as the boost provided by newer products fades. As a result, I am fairly neutral on Fastly's prospects now that its revenue multiple is back above 4x.

data.title: Texas Pacific Land Pivots Towards Long-Term Data Center Strategy
data.description:
Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL) may become a critical component to the value chain for large-scale data center buildouts as the firm aggregates services for sourcing power, water, and land. Through a recent equity investment in Bolt Data & Energy, the firm is positioning itself to accelerate data center development by creating a unified solution for sourcing power and infrastructure, potentially streamlining the process to begin taking on additional power and infrastructure-related royalties. Though this is an appealing opportunity for the organization, I believe this will be a multiyear process that may not begin to materialize until closer to the end of the decade. Given the near-term growth opportunity at hand, I am rating TPL shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $475/share at 33.05x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Texas Pacific Land Corporation Operational Update

TPL closed FY25 with strong production growth with total oil equivalent produced growing by 29% to 12.6MMboe, or 34.6Mboe/d. In q4’25, TPL realized a strong increase in daily volumes, reaching 37.5Mbbl/d, up from 36.30Mbbl/d in the previous quarter. Accordingly, TPL added 19.5 net wells for a total of 116.1 net producing wells as of the close of q4’25.

Despite the strong growth in q4’25, the oil and gas market may be facing certain challenges in the near-term as oil prices remain subdued. Despite the challenging price environment, production in the Permian Basin remains strong as producers more heavily leverage enhanced oil recovery tactics to flatten the decline curve and elongate mature wells.

Maturity of the basin also shows in TPL’s production volumes with oil volumes declining from 43% of total production down to 39% from 2023-2025.

The shift in production mix has modestly impacted TPL’s revenue generation, resulting in royalties per barrel of equivalent [BOE] declining to $28/boe in q4’25, down from $33/boe in the previous quarter. As a result of this and the winter freeze in January 2026, I suspect eq1’26 could be a challenging quarter for TPL. The freeze may have also impacted TPL’s water business for the quarter.

Water volumes also increased substantially with produced water royalties growing by 25% to 1.56Bbbl for FY25.

TPL is heavily pivoting into facilitating data center development through a $50mm investment in Bolt Data & Energy, a data & energy infrastructure company co-founded by Eric Schmidt, former CEO & Chairman of Google. As part of the agreement, TPL received its respective equity interest, warrants, and a right to first refusal to supply water to projects and infrastructure led by Bolt.

Bolt is a technology-first organization that provides a number of services for data integration and utilization for both digital and connected IoT. The basis of the TPL investment is to accelerate the rate of data center development in the Midland Basin, bringing together data center, infrastructure, and power assets as a unified service. This partnership can be exceptionally appealing given the power constraints in the US that’s creating some headwinds to the development of large-scale data centers. I believe this partnership can facilitate faster ramp-up, particularly given TPL’s direct ties to the oil & gas and water markets, creating a single-source solution for the data center developers. In accordance with TPL’s business model, Bolt will be responsible for facilitating these services.

As part of the data center theme, TPL leases two critical resources for data center operations: natural gas and water. Natural gas is set to be the go-to resource for power production over the coming years in conjunction with renewable energy resources before the potential shift to nuclear power. Data center operators are taking multiple approaches to sourcing load capacity to power, whether they’re connected to the grid or through behind-the-meter operations.

One of the differentiators is companies like Chevron Corp. (CVX) entering the power production market. Accordingly, Chevron reported its intent to purchase 7 gas turbines from GE Vernova (GEV) to generate 1GW of power capacity for large-scale data centers. Though the locations have yet to be set, I believe Chevron will likely partner with hyperscalers in selecting a region closest to the source of natural gas production, which may be in the Permian Basin or Bakken given its large production presence in the regions. TPL and Bolt may partner with companies like Chevron that can offer a vertically integrated approach to gas sourcing and power production, particularly given that Chevron’s power business may not be regulated as would a utility.

A behind-the-meter deal could be appealing to TPL’s royalties business, adding an additional, durable revenue stream that may not be influenced by the broader production market dynamics. If natural gas is produced in-basin with in-basin power production, TPL would essentially have a guaranteed revenue stream given the need for a consistent stream of electricity to power a data center. Though deal flow may take years to solidify and commence, I believe this business has the potential to decouple TPL from the supply/demand dynamics of the O&G industry.

Sourcing water may tie into this theme perfectly given the water intensity of direct-liquid cooling server racks. Large-scale data centers are estimated to consume upwards of 5MM gallons per day. Given the necessity of ultraclean water for data center operations, I believe TPL’s water business may be a critical feature for shoring up data center customers.

Overall, TPL is positioning itself to provide virtually all critical resource aggregation to operate the standing facilities in combination with its equity investment in Bolt. With the opportunity to transition more into an annual recurring revenue model, I believe the investment proposition is appealing and may potentially drive substantial cash flows in the coming years. The caveat is timing. Securing power equipment may be a major hangup given the shortage of electrical components.

Texas Pacific Land Corporation Operational Financial Position

Looking ahead to eq1’26, I’m forecasting TPL to generate $195mm in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $1.62/share. My forecast assumes some production disruption as a result of the Winter Storm in January 2026 along with moderating oil and natural gas prices. Looking ahead through the fiscal year, I’m forecasting production to continue to grow for both TPL’s O&G and water businesses, driving stronger margins as production scales.

Risks Related to Texas Pacific Land Corporation Operational

Bull Case

TPL’s long-term data center strategy may decouple operations from O&G market cycles. Though I believe that this may take some time to firmly begin earning royalties on these types of projects, long-term value generation can provide stable and durable cash flows.

Bear Case

Winter weather in January 2026 may impact production in eq1’26. Declining price per barrel of oil has influenced top-line growth in recent quarters and may persist if oil prices remain pressured.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

TPL increased the cash dividend in q4’25 by 12.5% to $0.60/share for a forward rate of $2.40/share. Despite the substantial distribution increase, TPL shares’ dividend yields 0.54%, making this less of an income stock for investors. Rather than valuing shares on income, I believe the growth opportunity at hand can result in share price appreciation over the years, particularly as the business transitions into power distribution and data center royalties.

Fundamentally, I believe TPL shares should be priced at $475/share at 33.05x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA. Given the potential shift in business in the coming years, I am recommending TPL with a Buy rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Southern Company: Hold Because Of Overvaluation In Utilities
data.description:
You can be a great company, and by no "fault" of your own, you can be part of a continued overvaluation that punishes the investment potential of your common stock. When I began investing in the NA stock market many years ago (back when doing so was still a lot more complicated for EU investors), utilities like Southern Company (SO) were of great interest to me. The reasons were simple: good dividend yield, very solid operations, very good safety, and one of the biggest players in their area.

Utilities, as a whole, are also fairly easily understood, no matter where they are - providing electricity and sometimes heat or other ancillary services. Very commonly, they are regulated by state or government authorities, which put caps on how much they are allowed to earn in returns for their operations or assets. And this is usually enough to balance a good valuation and a good return at a good valuation.

They are boring companies, but they give a lot better returns than, say, a savings account - at least usually.

Then you have times when utilities are either not liked or they are "too liked" - undervaluation and overvaluation, respectively, follow.

In this article, Southern Company is the focus. Structurally, it has the same approach as many US-based utilities. It's a holding company for multiple state or multi-state subsidiaries, the largest of which is Georgia Power. Its name suggests what it does, and where it does it.

I've covered a lot of utilities over the past year. My outlook on them has been very similar for most of them, also. Simply put, I've said that the combination of AI-fueled energy and infrastructure demand, coupled with massive investments into that infrastructure generation/grid, in turn, combined with their fundamentals, has created an unfavorable cycle. What I mean by "unfavorable cycle" is that the valuation due to the AI-led demand has seen valuations rise, and assumptions of significant earnings growth have been put into play (sometimes as much as double digits). At the same time, these utilities have massive CapEx plans, for some of them as much as their entire market capitalization rates for the coming 4-5 years, that supposedly will add earnings to their bottom line and improve growth.

What I generally believe investors to be missing is either overestimating growth rates from that CapEx and/or overestimating what the regulators will allow those utilities to earn, because what we're talking about are publicly facing utilities where costs are borne by "voters." This presents a risk.

Then there's the risk of AI growth not being as much as expected - but we'll get into that later.

For now, Southern Company.

Southern Company - Overvaluation gets even to the biggest companies

I believe Southern Company to be overvalued, and significantly so. Perhaps this is giving away the "punchline" a bit early, but let me show you why. The company's recent 4Q25 results do not change my view on this.

Southern Company is one of the largest utilities, and one of the utilities that has gone through significant changes for the past 10-20 years. About 20 years ago, the company's energy was almost 80% generated using coal. Over time, the share has dropped below 20%. This is possibly one of the largest changes in generation and assets to any US-based utility. The current company plans include closing down/retiring all but 8 remaining assets using coal within the next 24 months by increasing both straight renewable generation and increasing things like natgas.

But these ambitions and plans have been far from problem-free. The company's efforts have been marred by things like construction delays and cost overruns. The asset in Vogtle nuclear exemplifies this rather well. When the assets were finally finished about 18-20 months ago, they were five years behind schedule, and the cost overrun was over 90% for the company's ~46% ownership stake. Not exactly a good number. And if this is going to be the rule of thumb for its CapEx investments (not saying that it is, I'm saying if), then the upside obviously is not as high as bullish analysts would like to paint. This is only one example. For other execution issues, I would point to Kemper in Mississippi (a planned "clean coal asset"), which was over 30 months behind schedule with a more than 100% increase in budget - and that was before Southern decided to execute on this project as a more traditional sort of natgas plant in order for a tax benefit.

That is not to say that Southern, from a historical perspective, is a "bad executor". Quite the opposite. It's a great company in terms of execution. Management's targets of 5-7% are neither unrealistic nor unexpected, and I would generally say that this is good for a utility, especially of this size. The company also has a very solid history of hitting its stated forecasts. Fundamentals are good. Yield is there, and the company is A-rated despite what I, above 60%, consider to be an elevated long-term debt to capital, even before much of its expansion and investment plans. At the right price, this company is a very solid income investment with the potential for real growth over time.

What's more, I consider Southern to have perhaps one of the most constructive/best regulatory relationships of any large US utility. This is due to the low-energy rates as well as good operational history. The increasing demand for electricity due to the rising appeal of these geographies for business further supports the upside here. So my problems aren't with the company's fundamentals - my problems are with where the company is going, and what it's being priced at while doing so.

The company very recently reported its 4Q25/full-year results. It managed results along the higher end of its expected spectrum - $4.3/share in AEPS. Even before the company reported full-year results, though, I'd argue that this wouldn't have mattered for my thesis. It also doesn't matter that the company is seeing increases in demand from both residential and data centers. Not only do I consider these trends to be priced into the stock here, but I also consider them to be excessively priced in above any share price of $85/share.

The company's equity needs and financing needs for its growth projects are also a going concern, and 4Q25 has brought no significant news insofar as this goes. Currently, $2B of financing needs to be addressed, and the company has already pledged over $1.5B internally, billions in ATM and Hybrids, while maintaining its target of 17% FFO to debt.

Aside from this, I want to highlight a couple of challenges in what I saw as an otherwise solid operational year. First off, 4Q25 missed the mark (the full year didn't) by about 2 cents, or a little over 2%. A small thing, but might mark pressure. The same pressure is highlighted by increases in costs, specifically in maintenance and other "non-fuel" operational areas. There was also a non-trivial line item - a loss of $63M pre-tax for SO gas for capital investment disallowances by the ICC (Illinois in this context), which highlights regulatory challenges. An investment disallowance, in this context, is what it sounds like - the regulator, or PUC, does not allow a company to recover investment costs (in this case from consumers, the ones who pay the rate). It means essentially that the PUC - ICC in this case - doesn't allow those $63M to be included in that rate base.

I believe this serves as an indicator for further operational and regulatory risk, which management also highlighted in the earnings call. Wolfe Research and Goldman pointed out zoning and affordability concerns for one of the core geography (Georgia) data center prospects. This has also included some recent pieces of legislation that could impact things, called Senate Bill 436/House Bill 1059 (and that are not necessarily positive). If these are passed, that's a 3-year study plan.

What I am saying for the company is essentially the following.

I believe that, based on the company's execution for its growth projects in the past, especially big growth projects (which is what the company is looking to do over the next few years), there is a non-zero chance of significant cost overruns and miscalculations, as well as regulatory challenges that are not currently priced in.

Southern Company may have one of the better growth potentials in the segment, but this does not, in my view, remove the risk here (it increases it). I believe investors are significantly overestimating the earnings growth potential relative to these historically proven risks. The company does have a moat due to its business model and operations, but most utilities have some sort of moat due to what amounts to a regional monopoly for their services. The assumption that Southern will earn its cost of capital is not a strange one, but it's also not one that would warrant a significant premium.

And a significant premium is where we are currently at.

Valuation for Southern Company - not a great valuation in the case of project pressure

As I mentioned, I believe the right choice is to not invest here because I believe the company may drop going forward. The increased volatility is something that has become clearer to me, looking at the past 5 years, relative to the past 20.

The above trend reflects an assumption that the company is somehow worth much more than your standard 15x P/E. I view this assumption to be erroneous. I believe any fair value target or estimate above $80-$85/share for Southern has extreme difficulty in justifying itself.

The first reason for that target is the company's capital investment plan, which was recently increased to $76B from $63B. The current estimate essentially assumes solid execution in accordance with that, while precluding any failures and challenges that would result in cost overruns. This does not reflect history or sector trends. Assume for a moment that $76B in CapEx becomes $140B, exceeding the company's current market capitalization. This would not be irrational given the last major projects that Southern executed, but that became significantly more expensive. A hit of that sort would seriously mar the company's leverage. The company's new equity/debt will significantly slow earnings growth until these new operations are brought online. You're investing in the assumption that they will be done as expected and that the growth will be as expected.

While it may be, I believe this is too risky. Existing assets are set to grow EPS - Natgas, especially, is slated to grow nicely, but this represents less than 15% of current earnings.

A 20-year average P/E for the company comes to no more than 17x P/E. This would imply a fair value for 2028E, inclusive of the higher-end AEPS growth (which is higher than the historical rate) of about 7%, of about $90/share - but this is in 2 years. For 2026, the FV comes to about $79.8/share, which I put at $80/share. The PT for that would be about $72/share for a 15% annualized upside, inclusive of the dividend.

That means that the company is overvalued here, and the upside to the stated FV is less than 10% per year - in fact, below 2% - even including the dividend.

So, while I believe the company is a solid one, and there certainly is something to be said for good returns and safety at the right price.

Risks for this company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The probably biggest risk for the company's operations, aside from poor investment results and cost overruns, is deterioration of its relationship with its regulators - especially the bigger ones in states like Atlanta and Georgia. While this is recently settled thanks to a regulatory settlement, I believe there will be more factors outside of the company's control that contribute to less than superb results here. The by far biggest risk is the company's cost overruns for its CapEx. This should be considered in the context, however, that many of these projects are not bigger ones like a nuclear plant, but rather smaller ones. The cost overrun potential for smaller projects is historically significantly less than for bigger ones. So the CapEx rise estimate of 100% is considered somewhat extreme - more likely it could be half, or a portion of that. But it's still a risk - and given the company's leverage, interest cost, and especially valuation, it's something to keep in mind.

The thing to focus on is that at the right valuation, this company is a buy - but this is not the right valuation, and I don't see it going there quickly without a drop. That's why I'm saying not to invest at this price.

Thesis

The fundamentals for Southern Company are very solid. At the right sort of price, this company becomes a must-buy or must-own - however you want to call it.

Currently, there are two primary factors preventing this company from being a buy, and why I believe the right choice is to be neutral. First off, the company's CapEx plan, like other utility CapEx plans, is of significant execution risk and negative potential given its scope and reliance on what I would consider to be unconfirmed upside trends. I'd also add the risks of execution in the context of rising data center scrutiny.

When you take valuation into this context, and specifically the company's elevated valuation of over 21x P/E, which marks not only a significant premium to historical multiples but to anything I consider valid here, it makes investing at over a fair value of $80/share very difficult. I do, however, consider the PT/FV-relationship very tight and attractive here, and with a PT of around $72/share, it is a good entry here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $72/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of February 2026.

data.title: The Outlook For S&P 500 Dividends In February 2026
data.description:
The outlook for the S&P 500's dividends generally improved since the previous snapshot of their future. For the five quarters in our forecast window, four showed solid gains, while one had a small decline.

Here is our summary of how the outlook for the S&P 500's quarterly dividends per share has changed in the past month for the current quarter of 2026-Q1, the remaining three quarters of 2026, and the first quarter of 2027:

  • 2026-Q1: Increase of $0.21, rising to $21.71 per share
  • 2026-Q2: Decrease of $0.03, dipping to $19.70 per share
  • 2026-Q3: Increase of $0.24, rising to $20.33 per share
  • 2026-Q4: Increase of $0.40, rising to $20.42 per share
  • 2027-Q1: Increase of $0.27, rising to $21.60 per share

The following chart shows how expectations for the S&P 500's quarterly dividends per share changed in the month from 15 January 2025 to 13 February 2026.

As a general rule, the further out the forecast, the more subject to change the dividends will be before they are finalized at the end of their indicated quarter. Be sure to read the following section providing more information about dividend futures data to get a better understanding of what this information represents.

More About Dividend Futures Data

For this series, we take a snapshot of the CME Group's S&P 500 quarterly dividend futures data shortly after the second or third week of each month.

Dividend futures indicate the amount of dividends per share to be paid out over the period covered by each quarter's dividend futures contracts, which start on the day after the preceding quarter's dividend futures contracts expire and end on the third Friday of the month ending the indicated quarter. For example, as determined by dividend futures contracts, the now "current" quarter of 2026-Q1 began on Saturday, 20 December 2025, and will end on Friday, 20 March 2026, one month from today.

Because dividend futures are tied to options contracts that run on this schedule, that makes these figures different from the quarterly dividends per share figures that are reported by Standard & Poor's. S&P reports the amount of dividends per share paid out during regular calendar quarters after the end of each quarter. This term mismatch accounts for the differences in dividends reported by both sources, with the biggest differences between the two typically seen in the first and fourth quarters of each year.

How changes in the outlook for dividends at specific points of time in the future contribute to changes in current-day stock prices is described by this math.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: LCI Industries: Thriving In A Slow Industry Setting (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
LCI Industries (LCII) reported the company’s Q4 results on the 18th of February. The RV component manufacturer continued to report an earnings recovery regardless of challenging industry conditions. LCI’s results clearly reflect good market share gains in sales and efficiency gains in margins, setting up a good foundation for the next few years. An industry recovery is still on uncertain grounds, but poses clear longer-term upside for LCI.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my previous November 2023 article on the stock, titled “LCI Industries: Temporary Headwinds Cloud Long-Term Earnings Level”. The stock has since returned 38%, underperforming the S&P 500’s 58% gain.

LCI Q4 Review: Strong Operational Performance Amid Industry Weakness

LCI’s Q4 continued the recovery from a previous earnings drought. Revenues came in at $933 million, up by 16% year-on-year. Sales to RV manufacturers improved by 17% through strong pricing and market share gains, other end markets’ OEM revenues showed an even sharper 21% increase primarily due to acquisitions, and aftermarket revenues showed more stable growth at 8%.

Improved sales volume is quickly accretive to LCI’s earnings level. Operating income more than doubled to $35 million for the quarter, reflecting revenue growth and a significant 180 basis point gain in the operating margin to a 3.8% level. The gross margin improved by 100 basis points to 22.1%, and SG&A leverage enabled the remaining improvement in the operating margin. LCI has been very prudent in its overhead cost management, and has consolidated its manufacturing facilities for better efficiency, clearly showing up in the Q4 results on top of better sales volume.

I believe that the overall results were highly impressive. Industry conditions were still very challenging during Q4. RV wholesale units declined by -3.0% year-on-year across the industry, being in very sharp contrast to LCI’s results. LCI’s new products have been successful in enabling the company to capture market share profitably.

The results also came above Wall Street’s expectations. The topline beat the consensus estimate by $20 million, and the quarter’s adjusted EPS of $0.89 beat by $0.19.

Putting the quarter into a longer-term context, LCI is still largely recovering amid challenging industry conditions. While earnings are already past the company’s pre-Covid level, extensive acquisitions and market share gains are mostly behind the improvement. Industry-level demand is still very subdued; there’s still clear further recovery potential from an industry perspective. U.S. RV shipments grew by just 2.5% to 342.2 thousand for 2025, comparing to 406.1 thousand in 2019 and 483.7 thousand in 2018 in more normal demand conditions.

CEO Jason Lippert noted in the Q4 earnings call that the company expects a normal run-rate shipment level to be 375-415 thousand over the near term, and that RV’s are a “500 thousand-plus industry” over the long term. Assuming a recovery to 450 thousand in annual RV wholesale shipments over the next few years, there would be 31% industry-led growth potential in LCI’s primary RV OEM segment.

Such sales rebound potential would also likely be accretive to margins. LCI targets a 10% operating margin over the long term, comparing to 6.8% in 2025.

Ultimately, recovery expectations need to be cooled down for now. LCI expects industry-level RV wholesale shipments at just 335-350 thousand for 2026, stagnant from 2025 at the mid-point. High interest rates and a cautious consumer sentiment, and heightened consumer demand during 2021-2022 that likely weighs on current demand, continue to pressure retail-level sales. RV retailer Camping World Holdings’ (CWH) revenues haven’t shown a major recovery. An industry recovery likely won’t happen fast.

LCI’s financial guidance for 2026 suggests a continued earnings recovery regardless of industry challenges. Revenues are guided to come in at $4.2-4.3 billion, up by 3.1% at the mid-point. Market share gains continue to boost growth. The operating margin is guided to expand by 70-120 basis points, reflecting continued efficiency gains and prudent cost control.

LCI’s Valuation Is Attractive

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for LCI. I estimate elevated revenue growth through the 2027-2030 period, assuming a gradual industry recovery closer to 400 thousand annual units. Ultimately, I expect LCI’s revenue growth to remain stable at 2.5% after 2030, reflecting a fairly good market share performance and stable industry demand.

I now estimate the EBIT margin to stabilize at 9% in the next few years. Improved efficiency and higher sales volume should continue to push the margin level closer to LCI’s 10% target. The target level assumes divestments of low-margin businesses, and I only estimate LCI’s organic performance.

The cash flow conversion should be fair after taxes; amortization from previous acquisitions is a clear non-cash drag on EBIT. LCI does tend to invest quite heavily in growth through acquisitions, but I don’t assume any acquisitions in the DCF model.

The estimates put LCI’s fair value at $200.3, suggesting good 37% upside as of writing. I believe that the stock is attractively valued; LCI’s operational performance has been fantastic even in challenging industry conditions, and the company is positioned for further earnings upside once the industry recovers.

The fair value estimate is up from $100.7 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.87%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 4.11% interest rate, annualized from Q4 debt and interest expenses. I expect the company to leverage a good amount of debt in the future as well with a 30% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.08% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.49. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 10.61% and the WACC at 8.87%.

Risks

The investment still comes with clear risks. Most clearly, the macroeconomic environment continues to weigh on LCI; interest rates and other factors continue to pressure retail-level RV demand. Volatility in the consumer environment could cause earnings to grow slower than expected, or even decline, which could cause significant earnings revisions.

The bullish thesis is ultimately dependent on a fair recovery in the RV industry. While I believe that demand for RVs should ultimately recover closer to the pre-Covid level depending on macroeconomic conditions, consumer behavior could also be changing. Stable and high demand for RVs isn't guaranteed over the long term if consumers shift towards other recreational activities.

LCI has $945 million in long-term debt, making earnings more volatile and acting as an additional risk. I believe that LCI's level of debt is still manageable, though.

Takeaway

Industry conditions have remained challenging for LCI amid higher interest rates and weak consumer confidence, but the company’s results have been strong regardless. Market share gains, successful product launches, and operating efficiencies are showing up in earnings very clearly. An industry recovery would position LCI for significant further earnings growth, but a recovery doesn’t seem to be materializing in 2026 yet. I believe that LCI’s long-term earnings potential is now undervalued by the market; I upgrade my rating on LCII stock to Buy.
